entry.title: GMM
entry.content:
To estimate the unknown parameter vector $ _{K} $

Suppose there exist moment function \(\mathop{m_t(β)}\limits_{l\times1}\) (assume\(l\geq K\)) and \(E[m_t(\beta^o)]=0\) for unknown trueparameter \(\beta^o\)

We construct the sample analog as \(\mathop{\hat m(β)}\limits_{l\times1}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^n \mathop{m_t(β)}\limits_{l\times1}\)

Then the GMM estimator is \[\hat \beta =\arg \min_{\beta \in \Theta} \hat m(\beta) ' \hatW^{-1} \hat m(\beta)\] Remark Moment function may be nonlinear, thusGMM estimator may not have a closed form. But there is a special casewhen the moment function has the form \(m_t(β)= \mathop{Z_t}\limits_{l\times 1} (Y_t-X_t'\beta)\). Then,GMM estimator has the form like (Proof Required !)\[\hat \beta = (X'Z\hat W^{-1}Z'X)^{-1}X'Z\hat W^{-1}Z'Y\] 2SLS estimator and OLS estimator is the special case of thislinear IV estimator.

8.3 Consistency

We introduce 4 assumptions

  • Assumption 8.1 [Compactness]: Parameter space\(\Theta\) is compact set.

  • Assumption 8.2 [Uniform convergence]:

      1. moment function \(m_t(\beta)\) ismeasurable function for each \(β\in\Theta\), and given each \(t\),\(m_t(β)\) is continuous in \(β \in \Theta\).
      1. \(\bigl\{m_t(\beta)\bigr\}\) isstationary ergodic
      1. \(\hat m(β)\) convergencesuniformly over \(Θ\) to \(m(β)\equiv E(m_t(β))\), i.e. \[\sup_{\beta\in\Theta}|| \hat m(\beta) - m(\beta) || \xrightarrow{p}0\]
      1. \(m(β)\) is continuous in \(β \in \Theta\)

  • Assumption 8.3 [Specification]: exist uniquetrue parameter \(β^o\in\Theta\) suchthat \(m(β^o)=0\)

  • Assumption 8.4 [Weighting matrix]: ${ll}{ll} $ where \(W\) isnonstochastic, symmetric, finite and nonsingular.

Under Assumption 8.1 to 8.4, we can prove (not required) \[\hat \beta \xrightarrow{p} \beta^o\]

8.4 Asymptotic Normality

We further introduce two assumptions

  • Assumption 8.5 [Interirorness]: \(β^o \in int(Θ)\)

  • Assumption 8.6 [CLT]:

      1. for each \(t\), \(m_t(β)\) is continuously differentiablewith respect to \(β \in Θ\) w/p 1
      1. as \(n → \infty\)​​, \[\sqrt{n} \hat m(\beta^o)\equiv\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{t=1}nm_t(\beta^o) \xrightarrow{d} N(0,V_o) \text{\quad where } V_o \equivavar(\sqrt{n} \hat m(\beta^o))\]
      1. \(\left\{\frac{\partial}{\partial\beta}m_t(\beta)\right\}\)obey UWLLN, i.e. \[\sup_{\beta\in\Theta}\biggl|\biggl| \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^n\frac{\partial m_t(\beta)}{\partial \beta} - D(\beta)\biggl|\biggl| \xrightarrow{p}0 \\\text{where } D(\beta)\equiv E\biggl(\frac{\partial m_t(\beta)}{\partial\beta} \biggr) = \frac{\partial E[m_t(\beta)]}{\partial\beta}=\frac{\partial m(\beta)}{\partial \beta}\]

Under Assumption 8.1 to 8.6, we have (Proof Required!) \[\sqrt{n} (\hat\beta - \beta^o ) \xrightarrow{d} N(0,\Omega) \\ \text{where\quad } \Omega =(D_o'W^{-1}D_o)^{-1}D_o'W^{-1}V_oW^{-1}D_o(D_o'W^{-1}D_o)^{-1}\text{ and } D_o \equiv D(\beta^o)\]

8.5 Asymptotic Efficiency

Given moment function \(m_t(β)\), wewant to choose the optimal \(\hat W\)such that GMM estimator has minium variance.

Conclusion is that the optimal choice is (Proof Required!) \[W = V_0 \equiv avar[\sqrt{n}\hat m(\beta^o)]\] and corresponding variance is \[\Omega_o = (D_o' V_o^{-1} D_o)^{-1}\] Intuition is that, we assign smaller weights to the momentswhich have larger sampling variation

Remark: \(\hatβ_{2SLS}\) can be GMM estimator bychoosing \(\hat W = Z'Z\). Further,$_{2SLS} $ can be optimal GMM estimator under MDS and HMK.[proof required !]

But in practice, \(V_o\) is notavailable, so we can use its asymptotic estimator \(\hat W = \tilde V, \text{ where } \tilde V\xrightarrow{p}V_o\). So, we have the following two stepsGMM:

  • Step 1: Find a consistent estimator $β $​ by\[\tilde \beta = \arg\max_{\beta \in \Theta}\hat m(\beta)'\hatW^{-1}\hat m(\beta), \text{\quad where for convenience } \hat W = I\] and we use \(\tilde\beta\) toconstruct a asymptotic estimtor \(\tilde V\xrightarrow{p} V_o\). Construction depends on MDS:

    • Case I :\(\left\{m_t(β^o)\right\}\) isMDS

      In this case, \(V_o\equiv avar[\sqrt{n}\hatm(β^o)]=E[m_t(β^o)m_t(β^o)']\)

      we construct \(\tilde V\) as follows(Consistency Proof Required !) \[\tilde V = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}m_t(\tilde β)m_t(\tilde β)'\]

    • Case II :\(\left\{m_t(β^o)\right\}\) isnon-MDS

      in this case, \(V_o=\mathop{\sum}\limits_{j=-\infty}^{∞}Γ(j)\)where \(Γ(j)=cov[m_t(β_o),m_{t-j}(\beta_o)]=E[m_t(β_o)m_{t-j}(\beta_o)]\)

      we construct \(\tilde V\) as follows\[\tilde V = \sum_{j=1-n}^{n-1}k(j/p)\tilde \Gamma(j) \text{ where }\tilde \Gamma(j)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=j+1}^n m_t(\tilde\beta)m_{t-j}(\tilde\beta)'\]

  • Step 2: Choose \(\hatW = \tilde V\), then we can find optimal estimator \(\hatβ\): \[\hat\beta = \arg\min_{\beta\in\Theta} \hat m(\beta)'\tildeV^{-1}\hat m(\beta)\] and \[\sqrt{n}(\hat\beta -\beta^o) \xrightarrow{d}N(0,\Omega_o) \text{ where}\Omega_o=(D_o'V_o^{-1}D_o)^{-1}\]

8.8 Hypothesis Test

Consider testing null hypothesis \[\textbf{H}_0: R(\beta^o)=r\] where \(R(\cdot)\) is a \(J\times 1\) continuously differentiablefunction, which allows for nonlinear restriction. And we want to relate\(R(\hat β)-r\) with \(\sqrt{n}(\hat β-β^o)\).

By Taylor expansion we have \(\mathop{R(\hat β)}\limits_{J\times1}=\mathop{R(β^o)}\limits_{J\times 1}+\mathop{\frac{dR(\bar\beta)}{dβ}}\limits_{J\times K}\mathop{(\hat β -β^o)}\limits_{K\times1}\) where \(\barβ=λ\hatβ+(1-λ)β^o\).

Thus $$ \[\begin{align}\sqrt{n}[R(\hat\beta)-r] &=\sqrt{n}\mathop{[R(\beta^o)-r]}\limits_{^{H_0} = 0}+\frac{dR(\bar\beta)}{dβ}\mathop{\sqrt{n}(\hat\beta-\beta^o)}\limits_{\xrightarrow{d}N(0,\Omega_o)}\\&\xrightarrow{H_0}{N(0,\frac{dR(\beta^o)}{dβ}\Omega_o\frac{dR(\beta^o)}{dβ'})}\end{align}\] \[We can construct the T-test and Wald test statistic\] \[\begin{align}\text{when } J=1: \quad& T \equiv\frac{\sqrt{n}[R(\hat\beta)-r]}{\sqrt{\frac{dR(\hat\beta)}{dβ}\hat\Omega_o\frac{dR(\hat \beta)}{dβ'}}} \xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\\\text{when } J\geq1: \quad &W \equiv n [R(\hat\beta)-r]'\biggl[\frac{dR(\hat\beta)}{dβ}\hat\Omega_o\frac{dR(\hat\beta)}{dβ'}\biggr]^{-1}[R(\hat\beta)-r] \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_J\end{align}\] $$

8.9 Overidentification Test

For the null hypothetis test \[\text{H}_0: E[m_t(\beta^o)]=0 \text{\quad for some unknown }\beta^o\] Consider the sample analog \(\hatm(\hat \beta)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^nm_t(\hatβ)\) and see whetherit is significantly different from 0.

So, we have the following so called \(J\) test statistic [proof notrequired] \[J \equiv n \cdot \hat m(\beta)'\tilde V^{-1}\hat m(\beta)\xrightarrow{d}\chi^2_{l-K}\]

  • Remark 1: \(J\) test statistic onlyvalid when \(l\geq K\). (notice thedegree of freedom is \(l-K\))
  • Remark 2: \(J\) test statistic isactually "n times the value of step 2 GMM objective function".
  • Remark 3: we prefer a smaller \(J\), since the null hypothesis is IV isvalid.

Specially, under 2SLS procedures, we have so called Sargan'sTest **[Proof Required !]

entry.title: IV Regression
entry.content:
Consider the regression model, \(Y_t =X_t' \beta^o + \varepsilon_t\). Recall that the consistencyof $- ^o 0 $, requires the assumption $E(_t | X_t) =0 $. But, there arethree possibility that $E(_t | X_t) $.

  • Model misspecification (e.g. functional form misspecification oromitted variables)
  • Measurement error
  • Simultaneous equation bias

7.2 Assumption

  • Assumption 7.1 [Ergodic stationarity]: \(\left\{ Y_t, \mathop{X_t}\limits_{K\times 1}',\mathop{Z_t}\limits_{l\times 1}' \right\}_{t=1}^{'n}\) isan observable stationary ergodic process, and \(l \geq K\).
  • Assumption 7.2 [Linearity]: $ Y_t = X_t' _{K} +_t$
  • Assumption 7.3 [Nonsigularity]: \(\mathop{Q_{XX}}\limits_{K\timesK}=E(X_tX_t')\) is symmetric, finite and nonsingular.
  • Assumption 7.4 [IV conditions]:
      1. \(E(\varepsilon_t|X)\neq 0\)
      1. \(E(\varepsilon_t|Z_t)= 0\)
      1. \(\mathop{Q_{ZZ}}\limits_{l\timesl}=E(Z_tZ_t')\) and \(\mathop{Q_{ZX}}\limits_{l \timesK}=E(Z_tX_t')\) is finite and full rank
  • Assumption 7.5 [CLT]: \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\sum_{t=1}^{n}Z_t\varepsilon_t\xrightarrow{d} N(0, V)\) where \(\mathop{V}\limits_{K\times K}\equivavar(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\sum_{t=1}^{n}Z_t\varepsilon_t)\)

7.3 2SLS Estimation

Since \(E(X_t \varepsilon_t ) \neq0\), \(\hat \beta\) is notconsistent. But if we could decomposes \(X_t\) into two component \(X_t = \tilde X_t + v_t\), where \(\tilde X_t = \gamma' Z_t\) is thepopulation projection, and thus orthogonal to \(\varepsilon_t\). Then, we can still obtainthe consistent estimator of \(\beta^o\): \[\begin{align}Y_t &= X_t' \beta^o + \varepsilon_t \\&= \tilde X_t' \beta^0 + \underbrace{(v_t' \beta^o +\varepsilon_t)}_{u_t}\end{align}\] Notice \(E(\tilde X_t u_t) =\gamma'E(Z_tu_t)=\gamma'E(Z_tv_t')\beta^o +\gamma'E(Z_t\varepsilon_t)=0\), so this estimator isconsistent to \(\beta^o\). But \(\gamma'\) is unknown, \(\tilde X_t\) is unobservable, so we use\(\hat X_t = \hat \gamma' Z_t\) torepresent \(\tilde X_t\), where \(\hat \gamma'\) is the OLS estimator of\(X_t\) on \(Z_t\).

Thus, we have the following 2SLS procedures:

  • Stage 1: Estimate the model \[\mathop{X_t}\limits_{K\times 1} = \mathop{\gamma'}\limits_{K\timesl} Z_t + \mathop{v_t}\limits_{K\times 1} \text{ or } X = Z \gamma + v\] And store the fitted value, we have \[\begin{align*}\hat \gamma &= (Z'Z)^{-1}Z'X \\\hat X &= Z(Z'Z)^{-1}Z'X\end{align*}\] We actually run \(K\)regressions: \((X_1, \cdots, X_K)=Z(\gamma_1,\cdots, \gamma_K)+(v_1,\cdots, v_K)\).

  • Stage 2: Estimate the model \[Y_t = \hat X_t' \beta^o + \hat u_t \text{ or } Y = \hat X \beta^o\] we have \[\begin{align*} \hat \beta_{2SLS} &= (\hat X' \hat X)^{-1}\hat X' Y \\ &= \bigl( X'Z(Z'Z)^{-1}Z'X\bigr)^{-1}X'Z(Z'Z)^{-1}Z'Y\end{align*}\]

7.4 Consistency

Under Assumption 7.1-7.4, [Proof Required !] \[\hat\beta_{2SLS} \xrightarrow{p} \beta^o\]

7.5 Asymptotic Normality

Under Assumption 7.1-7.5, [Proof Required !] $$\[\begin{align}&\qquad \sqrt{n}(\hat \beta_{2SLS} - \beta^o) \xrightarrow{d} N(0,\Omega) \\\text{where }&\Omega = \bigl( Q_{XZ} Q_{ZZ}^{-1} Q_{ZX} \bigr)^{-1}Q_{XZ} Q_{ZZ}^{-1} V Q_{ZZ}^{-1} Q_{ZX} ( Q_{XZ} Q_{ZZ}^{-1} Q_{ZX}\bigr)^{-1} \\\text{and }& V \equiv avar\biggl(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\sum_{t=1}^nZ_t\varepsilon_t\biggr)\end{align}\] $$

  • Case I: \(\bigl\{Z_t\varepsilon_t\bigr\}\)MDS+HMK

    In this case, $$ \[\begin{align}V \equiv& avar\biggl(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\sum_{t=1}^n Z_t\varepsilon_t\biggr) \mathop{=}\limits^{\text{MDS}} E(Z_t Z_t' \varepsilon_t^2)\mathop{=}\limits^{\text{HMK}} \sigma^2 E(Z_t Z_t') \\\Omega =& \sigma^2 \bigl( Q_{XZ} Q_{ZZ}^{-1} Q_{ZX} \bigr)^{-1}\end{align}\] \[Asymptotic variance estimator is\] & S^2 e' e = {t=1}^{n} e_t^2 ^2, e = Y - X {2SLS}\

    & = S^2 ( Q_{XZ} Q_{ZZ}^{-1} Q_{ZX} )^{-1} \[Wald test statistic is\] W = ^2_J
    $$

  • Case II: \(\bigl\{Z_t\varepsilon_t\bigr\}\)MDS+HTK

    In this case, \[V = E(Z_tZ_t'\varepsilon_t^2)\] Asymptotica variance estimator is \[\hat V \equiv \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^n Z_tZ_t'\hat e_t^2\xrightarrow{p} E(Z_t Z_t' \varepsilon_t^2) \\\hat \Omega \equiv \bigl( \hat Q_{XZ} \hat Q_{ZZ}^{-1} \hat Q_{ZX}\bigr)^{-1} \hat Q_{XZ} \hat Q_{ZZ}^{-1} \hat V \hat Q_{ZZ}^{-1} \hatQ_{ZX} (\hat Q_{XZ}\hat Q_{ZZ}^{-1} \hat Q_{ZX} \bigr)^{-1}\] Robust Wald test statistic is \[W_r = n (R\hat \beta_{2SLS}-r)'(R \hat \Omega R')^{-1} (R\hat\beta_{2SLS}-r)\]

  • Case III: \(\bigl\{Z_t\varepsilon_t\bigr\}\)non-MDS

    In this case, $ V = _{k=-}^{}(j)$

    We directly assume the asymptotic variance estimator \(\hat V \xrightarrow{p} V\)

    Robust Wald test under conditional heteroskedasticity andautocorrelation is \[W_r = n (R\hat \beta_{2SLS}-r)'(R \hat \Omega R')^{-1} (R\hat\beta_{2SLS}-r)\]

entry.title: Stationary Time Series & HAC estimator
entry.content:
• Assumption 5.1 [Ergodic Stationarity]: \(\left\{ Y_t, X_t'\right\}_{t=1}^{'n}\) is jointly stationary and ergodic
  • Assumption 5.2 [Linearity]: \(Y_t = X_t' \beta^o +\varepsilon_t\)
  • Assumption 5.3 [Correct Model Specification]: \(E(\varepsilon_t|X_t)=0\) with \(E(\varepsilon_t^2)=\sigma^2<\infty\)
  • Assumption 5.4 [Nonsingularity]: \(Q=E(X_tX_t')\) is symmetric, finite andnonsingular
  • Assumption 5.5 [MDS]: \(\left\{X_t\varepsilon_t\right\}\) is MDSwith respect to the \(\sigma\)-fieldgenerated by \(\left\{X_t\varepsilon_t,s<t\right\}\) and \(\mathop{V}\limits_{K\times K}\equivvar(X_t\varepsilon_t)=E(X_tX_t'\varepsilon_t^2)\) is finiteand PD.

5.3 Consistency

For proof, we introduce WLLN for Ergodic StationaryProcess

  • If \(\left\{ Z_t \right\}\) isa stationary ergodic process with \(E(Z_t)=\mu\) and \(E|Z_t|<\infty\). Then \(\bar Z_n \xrightarrow{p} \mu\)

Under Assumption 5.1-5.4, \[\hat \beta - \beta^o \xrightarrow{p} 0\]

5.4 Asymptotic Normality

For proof, we introduce CLT for Ergodic StationaryMDS

  • If \(\left\{ Z_t \right\}\) isstationary ergodic MDS with \(var(Z_t)=V\) finite, symmetric and positive definite. Then as\(\sqrt{n} \bar Z_n \xrightarrow{d}N(0,V)\)
HTK

Under Assumption 5.1-5.5, \[\sqrt{n} (\hat \beta - \beta^o ) \xrightarrow{d} N(0,Q^{-1}VQ^{-1})\]

HMK

Under Assumption 5.1-5.5 and conditional homoskedasticity \(E(\varepsilon_t^2|X_t)=\sigma^2\)(Assumption 5.6) \[\sqrt{n} (\hat \beta - \beta^o ) \xrightarrow{d} N(0,\sigma^2Q^{-1})\]

5.5 Asymptotic VarianceEstimator

HMK

Under Assumption 5.1-5.5, \[s^2 \xrightarrow{p}\sigma^2\] and thus \[s^2\hat Q^{-1} \xrightarrow{p} \sigma^2Q^{-1}\]

HTK

Under Assumption 5.1-5.5 and \(E(X_{jt}^4)<\infty\) for \(0\leq j \leq k\) and \(E(\varepsilon_t^4)<\infty\) (Assumption5.7), \[\hat V \equiv \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^nX_tX_t'e_t^2 \xrightarrow{p} V\] and thus \[\hat Q^{-1} \hat V\hat Q^{-1} \xrightarrow{p}Q^{-1}VQ^{-1}\]

5.6 Hypothesis Testing

HMK

Under Assumption 5.1-5.5 and conditional homoskedasticity \(E(\varepsilon_t^2|X_t)=\sigma^2\)(Assumption 5.6), under \(\textbf{H}_0\)

$$ \[\begin{align}\text{when }J=1: \quad & T = \frac{R \hat \beta - r}{\sqrt{s^2} R(X'X)^{-1} R'} \xrightarrow{p} N(0,1) \\\text{when }J\geq1: \quad & W = J \cdot F = \frac{(R\hat \beta -r)'[R(X'X)^{-1}R']^{-1}(R\hat \beta - r)}{s^2}\xrightarrow{d} \chi_{J}^2\end{align}\] $$

HTK

Under Assumption 5.1-5.5 and 5.7, under \(\textbf{H}_0\)

$$ \[\begin{align}\text{when } J=1: \quad &T_r = \frac{\sqrt{n}(R\hat \beta -r )}{\sqrt{R \hat Q^{-1} \hat V \hatQ^{-1} R'}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0,1) \\\text{when } J\geq 1: \quad &W_r = n (R\hat \beta -r )' [R \hat Q^{-1} \hat V \hat Q^{-1}R']^{-1}(R\hat \beta -r ) \xrightarrow{d} \chi_{J}^2\end{align}\] $$

Chap 6 HAC Estimation

  • Assumption 6.1 [Ergodic stationarity]: \(\left\{ Y_t, X_t'\right\}_{t=1}^{'n}\) is an observable stationary ergodicprocess.
  • Assumption 6.2 [Linearity]: \(Y_t = X_t' \beta^o +\varepsilon_t\)
  • Assumption 6.3 [Correct model specification]: \(E(\varepsilon_t|X_t)=0\)
  • Assumption 6.4 [Nonsingularity]: \(\mathop{Q}\limits_{K\timesK}=E(X_tX_t')\) is symmetric, finite and nonsingular.
  • Assumption 6.5 [Long-run variance]:
      1. \(\sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} ||\Gamma(j)||< ∞\) where $ (j) cov(X_tt, X{t-j}_{t-j})$ and \(V = \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \Gamma(j)\)is PD
      1. $E(X_tt|X{t-j}{t-j},X{t-j-1}_{t-j-1},) 0 j $
      1. \(\sum_{j=0}^\infty[E(r_j'r_j)]^{1/2}<\infty\)Where \(r_j=E(X_t\varepsilon_t|X_{t-j}\varepsilon_{t-j},X_{t-j-1}\varepsilon_{t-j-1},\cdots)-E(X_t\varepsilon_t|X_{t-j-1}\varepsilon_{t-j-1},X_{t-j-2}\varepsilon_{t-j-2},\cdots)\)

Consistency

Under Assumption 6.1-6.5.a \[\hat \beta \xrightarrow{p} \beta^o\]

Asymptotic Normality

For proof, we introduce CLT for Zero Mean Ergodic StationaryProcesses

  • Suppose \(\left\{Z_t\right\}\)is a stationary ergodic process with
    • \(E(Z_t)=0\);
    • \(V = \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty}\Gamma(j)\) is finite and nonsingular, where \(\Gamma(j)=E(Z_tZ_{t-j}')\);
    • \(E(Z_t|Z_{t-j},Z_{t-j-1},\cdots)\xrightarrow{q.m.}0\);
    • \(\sum_{j=0}^\infty[E(r_j'r_j)]^{1/2}<\infty\)where \(r_j=E(Z_t|Z_{t-j},Z_{t-j-1},\cdots)-E(X_t\varepsilon_t|X_{t-j-1}\varepsilon_{t-j-1},X_{t-j-2}\varepsilon_{t-j-2},\cdots)\)

​ Then, \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \bar Z_n\xrightarrow{d} N(0,V)\)

Under Assumption 6.1-6.5 \[\sqrt{n}(\hat \beta -\beta^o) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, Q^{-1}VQ^{-1}) \text{where } V = \sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \Gamma(j)\]

Asymptotic VarianceEstimator

To estimate the asymptotic variance \(V =\sum_{j=-\infty}^{\infty} \Gamma(j)\), first consider a naiveestimator \[\hat V = \sum_{j=-(n-1)}^{n-1}\hat\Gamma(j)\] where \(\hat\Gamma(j)\xrightarrow{p}\Gamma(j)\), but \(\hatV \nrightarrow^p V\) since there are too many terms and variancemay explosive.

Then, we consider the truncated sum \[\hat V = \sum_{j=-p}^{p}\hat\Gamma(j)\] But when \(p\) is fixed, wecould expect \(\hat V\xrightarrow{p}\sum_{j=-p}^{p}\Gamma(j) \neq V\).

Hence, we should let \(p\)grows to infinity as \(n \rightarrow\infty\) but cannot grow as fast as sample size \(n\). Otherwise, the variance of\(\hat V\) will never vanish to zero.Therefore, to ensure consistency of \(\hatV\) to \(V\), we should balancethe bias and the vaiance of \(\hat V\)properly. Consider \[\hat V = \sum_{j=-p_n}^{p_n}\hat\Gamma(j), \text{ where } p_n\rightarrow \infty, p_n/n \rightarrow 0\] Although this estimator is consistent for \(V\), it may not be positive semi-definitefor all \(n\). So, use a weightedaverage estimator where weighting function \(k(j/p_n)\) is kernel function. \[\hat V = \sum_{j=-p_n}^{p_n}k(j/p_n)\hat\Gamma(j)\]

Hypothethis Test

Under Assuption 6.1-6.5 and directly assume \(\hat V \xrightarrow{p}V\) (Assumption 6.6),then under \(\textbf{H}_0\)

entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
Welcome to Hexo! This is your veryfirst post. Check documentation formore info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find theanswer in troubleshooting oryou can ask me on GitHub.

Quick Start

Create a new post

More info: Writing

Run server

More info: Server

Generate static files

More info: Generating

Deploy to remote sites

More info: Deployment
